title: Temporal vs. Spatial Dimension
concept: Videos contain both spatial dimensions (height and width of each image) and a temporal dimension (the progression of frames over time).

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the primary metric for video frame frequency?
exercises.explain: Yes! FPS measures the frequency of frames in video data.

cards.title: Where did the time go?
cards.body: When you look at a photograph of a runner, you can see their height and width. But you can't tell if they are starting to run or slowing down. To see speed or acceleration, you need a brand new coordinate: time.

cards.title: Spatial vs. Temporal
cards.body: Spatial dimensions refer to the space inside a single frame: its Height and Width (the pixels). The Temporal dimension refers to the Time axis (the frame sequence). Video models must learn from both.

cards.title: A book's pages
cards.body: Think of a comic book. A single page has spatial dimensions (height and width of the panel drawings). The sequence of pages is the temporal dimension. To understand the story, you need to read across the page (space) AND turn the pages (time).

cards.title: Analyzing a soccer kick
cards.body: To identify a soccer kick: Spatial features detect the ball, the player's leg, and the grass. Temporal features detect the leg moving backward, contacting the ball, and the leg swinging forward.

cards.title: Time is not just another space
cards.body: It's tempting to treat time as just a third spatial dimension (like depth). But space has no direction (you can go left or right), whereas time only flows forward! Models must handle time sequence order carefully.

cards.title: Comparing Dimensions
cards.body: Visualizing how spatial and temporal dimensions operate in video:

left.text:
Height & Width of frames
Detects objects, colors, textures
Answers: 'What is in the frame?'

right.text:
Frame indices over time
Detects speed, direction, action
Answers: 'What is happening?'

exercises.prompt: Which dimension captures the speed and direction of a moving car?
exercises.explain: Correct! Motion, speed, and direction occur across frames over the temporal (time) dimension.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An algorithm that only looks at a single static frame can extract temporal features.
exercises.explain: False. Temporal features require comparing multiple frames over time to detect changes.

exercises.sentence: Spatial features describe shapes and textures, while ___ features describe motion and actions.
exercises.explain: Temporal features represent how spatial features change from frame to frame.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a purely spatial feature in a video frame?

options.text: The red color of a stop sign
The flickering of a light bulb

exercises.explain: The color of a stop sign can be detected in a single static frame without time information.

exercises.prompt: A model is tasked with recognizing if someone is waving. If it only extracts spatial features, what is it likely to predict?

options.text: It can detect a raised hand, but cannot distinguish waving from holding a hand still.
It will perfectly detect the waving because waving is a static pose.
It will output an error because waving has no spatial features.
It will predict the exact velocity of the hand movement.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Spatial features can identify a hand and its position, but to classify the repetitive motion of waving, the model must track the hand's spatial coordinates over the temporal axis. Next: how do we structure this as numerical data?
